kofa/g-man forum exchange on 'help-with-highlight-reconstruction-of-clouds' is explicitly tagged confidence: hearsay and involves unconfirmed-as-core-dev forum participants; it should not be treated as adding independent authoritative weight beyond 'a data point consistent with the WB-precondition pattern' -- it is corroborating in direction only, not in evidentiary strength.

The claim about ACR/Camera Raw's internal pipeline ORDER for Defringe vs. highlight recovery is explicitly a documented dead end/absence of evidence ('n/a' quote, 'searched...' source) -- this is correctly represented as a gap, not a claim, and should not be cited as if Adobe's ordering is known.

Jim Kasson's M11 highlight-recovery measurements and his ETTR-headroom 'turning pink' claim are both sourced from blog posts whose direct fetch failed (403) and were reconstructed only from search-engine snippets -- confidence is self-labeled 'secondary' but the actual verification chain is weak (paraphrase-of-paraphrase, not verified against the primary blog text). Treat the specific EV numbers (ETTR+0.5/1.5/2 EV) as plausible but not verified quotations.

The scRGB claim is self-labeled 'hearsay' and explicitly notes the IEC PDF could not be text-extracted, resting only on Wikipedia; additionally the finding admits no evidence was found of scRGB's actual use in current mainstream photo pipelines, so the historical-precedent framing is sound but any implied present-day relevance is unsupported.

The 16-bit-sRGB-TIFF-has-no-headroom-paths claim is explicitly self-labeled 'hearsay' and described by the research itself as a deductive synthesis rather than something any single source states -- it is probably true as a logical deduction from the sRGB clip rule plus integer container bounds, but it is not an attested fact and shouldn't be cited as if a standards body said it.

The Adobe gain-map formula (recovered HDR = (SDR+offset)*exp2(...)) is sourced to a Google/Android implementation doc as a proxy for Eric Chan's original Adobe PDF, which itself failed to fetch (403) -- confidence 'secondary' is reasonable but this is a second-hand implementation's rendering of a spec, not the spec itself; exact formula details should be re-verified against the original Adobe gain-map spec before being treated as normative.

The ISO TS 22028-5 finding cites a color.org presentation PDF 'title/context only' with explicit acknowledgment the PDF body wasn't text-extractable -- the attribution to 'Nicolas Bonnier' and any specific content beyond the standard's title/scope should be treated as unconfirmed.

The Adobe Lightroom Journal Defringe two-mode (All Edges / Highlight Edges) claim could not be fetched directly (TLS/cert failure on the primary blogs.adobe.com source) and rests on secondary tutorial-site corroboration (Photofocus, ACDSee docs) -- reasonable secondary-tier grounding but not verified against Adobe's own text.

The LogLuv TIFF finding is well-sourced to the format's original author (Greg Ward, primary-source tier) for the technical specification, but the claim that it is 'effectively dead in mainstream photo-editing tooling today' is an inference from absence of search hits, not a positive finding -- should be treated as a plausible-but-unverified absence claim, not confirmed dead status.

Scott Dyer's ACEScentral forum post on negative pixel values is attributed 'secondary' but is really a single forum post from one named individual on a community forum (not an official ACES/Academy publication) -- treat as a knowledgeable individual's explanation rather than an official standards position, despite the useful technical content.

agreements: Cross-tool convergence on the physical root cause: clipping of one or more channels destroys true hue/chroma information, independently stated for darktable (Zhang & Brainard 2004 JOSA A; Rouf/Lau/Heidrich 2012 UBC), RawTherapee/ART (RawPedia Color Propagation docs; Beep6581 issue #3311), and general optics (patents US8582878B1 Zoran/CSR, US7577292B2 Microsoft; DPReview/Wikipedia synthesis) — at least 4 independent source families agree clipping-adjacent color contamination is the mechanism.

White-balance/white-point correctness as a precondition for correct highlight reconstruction is independently asserted by darktable's Hanno Schwalm (multiple threads: 'Removal of magenta highlight artefacts' discuss.pixls.us/32629, GitHub #13849 comments) AND independently by RawTherapee contributors in issue #3311/#6815 (iliasg's white-level-4094-vs-4095 fix; Desmis/Lawrence37 confirming WB is applied before Inpaint Opposed specifically because the algorithm is WB-sensitive) AND by the pixls.us cloud-tint thread (kofa/g-man, though that one is hearsay-tier) — three separate projects/threads agree WB error is a recurring confound in evaluating chroma-reconstruction artifacts.

Boundary/neighbor-based chroma estimation ('bleed' or 'propagate' color from unclipped surround into clipped region) is documented as the shared mechanism — and shared failure mode — across darktable segmentation/inpaint-opposed (user manual, PR #10716, PR #12960), RawTherapee Color Propagation (RawPedia, issue #3311), and the academic lineage (Rouf et al. 2012 Laplace/Dirichlet boundary hue interpolation; Xu/Doutre/Nasiopoulos 2011 region-based chroma correction; Elboher & Werman Color Lines) — at least 3 independent implementations plus 3 academic papers converge on 'estimate chroma from unclipped neighbors' as the dominant non-neutral reconstruction strategy, with the same 'wrong-color-bleed-in' artifact reported in both darktable (issue #13849 magenta cast, issue #12399 boundary exclusion) and RawTherapee (issue #3311 purple fringing) camps.

Segment/region-boundary hard edges as a distinct, separate artifact from color-bleed/magenta-cast is corroborated twice independently: GitHub #13849 (groutr's report: switching to segmentation removed magenta but produced a harsh boundary line at the segment edge) and GitHub #12399 (MStraeten's report of bounded clipped areas excluded from correction at default 'combine' settings, producing boundary artifacts) — both point to the same underlying segmentation/morphology parameter space (combine, candidating) as the lever, and PR #12960 and the user-manual 'candidating' documentation agree on the parameter's function.

Purple/magenta fringing being gated by clipping severity (invisible until the highlight clips to white) is stated by two independent lineages: the patent literature (Zoran/CSR US8582878B1 explicitly builds its Saturation-Score detector on 'purple fringing mostly occurs in pixels adjacent to saturated pixels', and Microsoft's US7577292B2 independently defines candidate fringe pixels via near-saturation adjacency) and the qualitative optics framing (DPReview/Wikipedia synthesis) — patent-level and optics-level sources agree on the same adjacency-to-saturation precondition.

Multiple independent 'raw, pre-color-management' architecture arguments agree that some clipped-highlight corrections must happen before white balance/input-profile are applied: darktable's Aurelien Pierre (issue #2086, issue #11587) argues LCh-domain correction is invalid post-demosaic/pre-profile, and separately that magenta detection is only reliable pre-profile; darktable's guided-laplacians docs independently state the module is designed to be 'immune to white-balance discrepancies' by working on raw data; RawTherapee's Desmis/Lawrence37 (issue #6815) independently confirm Inpaint Opposed needs WB applied first (opposite ordering claim but same underlying premise that WB-timing relative to reconstruction is architecturally significant) — three separate engineering teams converge on WB-ordering being a first-class design axis for highlight reconstruction, even though they land on different orderings.

Two independent format specifications agree that 1.0/white is not a hard clamp in scene-referred/HDR containers: OpenEXR technical docs (1.0 as nominal 100% reflector, not a clamp) and the DNG spec's LinearRaw/float photometric interpretation (unbounded by construction) — both corroborate that 'unbounded linear master' is an established still-image engineering pattern, not video-exclusive.

Negative/out-of-gamut and superwhite values being a normal, non-corrupt artifact of camera colorimetry (not signal error) is stated independently for ACES/EXR (ACEScentral thread, Scott Dyer) and implied structurally by the scRGB standard's explicit negative-value allowance (IEC 61966-2-2) — two independent sources agree extended range must accommodate real-world out-of-triangle camera signals, not just brightness overshoot.
conflicts: Direct, explicit conflict on which highlight-reconstruction method produces the BEST overall image quality: RawTherapee's Desmis (issue #6815) ranks Color Propagation > Inpaint Opposed > Blend on quality and states Inpaint Opposed was made default mainly for a quality/speed compromise, NOT because it's better — while darktable's own trajectory (PR #10716, #12960, #17190, #20690, and Schwalm's repeated troubleshooting posts) treats inpaint-opposed/segmentation as the actively-improved, most-trusted default path, with the older LCh method explicitly declared 'broken from scratch' by Aurelien Pierre (issue #2086) and RawTherapee's own Color Propagation independently declared unsafe on clipped-adjacent-to-different-color images by Beep6581 himself ('don't use color propagation on such images... by definition it propagates colors... suffer from purple fringing', issue #3311). This is a real cross-project conflict: the RawTherapee ranking (best=Color Propagation) is directly contradicted by RawTherapee's own project founder's warning against using that same method on the artifact-prone case, and by darktable's abandonment of the equivalent neighbor-bleed strategy as default. The Desmis quality ranking looks weaker-grounded (single-source, and self-contradicted within the same repo's issue tracker) versus the Beep6581/Schwalm consensus that boundary-color-bleed methods are failure-prone near clipped/differently-colored boundaries.

Conflicting account of WB-vs-reconstruction pipeline ORDERING across engines, though not necessarily contradictory once read carefully: RawTherapee's Inpaint Opposed applies WB BEFORE reconstruction (Desmis/Lawrence37, issue #6815 — confirmed by a refresh-mapper code fix), whereas darktable's guided-laplacians is designed to run without needing correct WB at all (operates pre-color-management, 'immune to white-balance discrepancies'), and darktable's LCh/segmentation methods per Aurelien Pierre operate 'before demosaicing... before the input colour profile is applied' yet Schwalm insists correct WB coefficients are still required as input for correct results (discuss.pixls.us/32629) — i.e. within darktable itself there is tension between 'designed to not need WB' (guided laplacians) and 'requires perfect WB as a precondition' (Schwalm's statements about the other algorithms in the same module). This is not a hard logical contradiction (different algorithms, different requirements) but the findings do not clearly reconcile why darktable ships multiple highlight-recon algorithms with opposite WB-dependency philosophies, and no source directly contrasts them against each other.

Minor internal conflict on segmentation's own tradeoffs: PR #10716/#12960/user-manual frame segmentation as the more sophisticated, boundary-aware, 'checks for valid colors at the borders' algorithm (Schwalm, 'Highlight reconstruction - hard edges' thread) recommending it for large blown-out areas — yet issue #13849 shows segmentation trading one artifact (magenta cast) for a different, clearly visible one (hard segment-boundary line), and issue #12399 shows segmentation's default parameters actively excluding valid bounded regions from correction. The 'segmentation is more robust/border-aware' framing from Schwalm's advocacy posts is in tension with the concrete bug reports showing segmentation's boundary handling is itself fragile and parameter-sensitive (combine, candidating) — the advocacy claims read as somewhat oversold relative to the bug-tracker evidence from the same maintainer's own repo.

Unresolved/unreconciled naming conflict: PR #10716 explicitly attributes the segmentation method's origin to Hanno Schwalm + 'Iain' from G'MIC with no mention of 'garagecoder', and states 'no corroborating evidence' was found for garagecoder's involvement (angle: pixls-segmentation) — but a later finding (angle: dt-github) cites the segbased.c source header as crediting 'Iain and garagecoder of the G'MIC team AND Hanno Schwalm.' These two findings directly disagree on whether garagecoder was involved; the source-code header (primary-source, closer to ground truth than a PR description) should probably be weighted over the earlier PR-based 'no corroborating evidence' claim, but the set never flags or resolves this contradiction explicitly — it is a real conflict between two primary-source claims in the same finding set.
unsupported: The Rouf/Lau/Heidrich mechanism description ('sensor clipping destroys hue... DCRAW fails to correct') is sourced to blog.kasson.com but that URL 403'd — the finding admits it's actually corroborated only via the academic PDF, meaning the Kasson-specific attribution is unconfirmed hearsay riding on a real primary source; treat the blog.kasson.com claim itself as unverified.

Both Jim Kasson blog findings (M11 ETTR+0.5/1.5/2EV highlight-recovery progression, and the 'blue clipping causes pink' ETTR headroom claim) are explicitly reconstructed from WebSearch snippets because direct fetch was blocked (403) — single-source, unverified against primary text, self-labeled 'secondary' but functionally closer to hearsay since the actual page content was never read.

The pixls.us cloud-tint thread (kofa/g-man, 'help-with-highlight-reconstruction-of-clouds') is explicitly labeled confidence:hearsay and involves unconfirmed forum participants (not verified core devs) — should not be used to support any claim about darktable's official position, only as anecdotal corroboration.

The RawPedia Defringe documentation claim is sourced via 'search-engine synopsis' because the page itself was unreachable (hosting outage) — single-source, unverified against primary text despite being labeled 'secondary'.
The scRGB claim (IEC 61966-2-2 range -0.5 to +7.5) rests on Wikipedia plus a 403'd IEC PDF — self-labeled hearsay, single tertiary source, no primary standard text actually read.

The 16-bit sRGB TIFF 'zero headroom' synthesis is explicitly self-described as a deductive synthesis with 'no single primary source states this directly' — labeled hearsay by the finding itself; treat as reasoning-by-the-researcher rather than a sourced fact, though the deduction itself is logically sound given the sRGB clip-rule primary source.

Adobe's internal pipeline ordering for Defringe vs. highlight recovery is an explicit dead-end/absence-of-evidence finding — correctly flagged as a gap, not a claim, but worth noting no source in the entire set actually answers the ACR-specific ordering question that parallel darktable/RT findings do answer for their own engines.

The Adobe gain-map math finding (recovered HDR formula) relies on Eric Chan's PDF failing to fetch (403), so the actual formula is corroborated only via Google's Android implementation doc describing Adobe's scheme secondhand — single independently-readable source for a fairly precise mathematical claim.

ISO/TS 22028-5 content and the Nicolas Bonnier ICC presentation are sourced to a PDF whose body 'was not text-extractable' — the claim rests on the standard's title/registry metadata only, not verified content.

The LogLuv TIFF finding, while primary-source (Greg Ward's own page), includes an unverified secondary claim that it has 'no evidence of current support in Photoshop/Lightroom' — this is an absence-of-evidence claim (nobody searched Adobe's actual format-support matrix), so the 'effectively dead' characterization is weakly supported.

The GitHub PR #20690 and PR #17190 technical claims (3x3 photosite geometry fix, calc_refavg border bug) are single-source (only the PR description itself), with no corroborating issue reports or independent testing cited to confirm the described bugs were actually user-visible before the fix — plausible given the author is the maintainer, but uncorroborated by a second party.
